trans-unit.source: Labels for Vector Data
trans-unit.target: Labels for Vector Data

trans-unit.source: Labels are text that display information or values of the data. In QGIS, you can either select __Single Labels__ or __Rule-based Labelling__. For each option, an attribute (`value`) will be displayed on the map. For example, the name of a city or region. Additionally, you can __change the font, font size, colour and some other styling options__ for the label text. When you create a map, you can add labels to help your reader understand the map quickly. However, be aware that too much text information can overload the map with too much information for the reader to process.
trans-unit.target: Labels are text that display information or values of the data. In QGIS, you can either select __Single Labels__ or __Rule-based Labelling__. For each option, an attribute (`value`) will be displayed on the map. For example, the name of a city or region. Additionally, you can __change the font, font size, colour and some other styling options__ for the label text. When you create a map, you can add labels to help your reader understand the map quickly. However, be aware that too much text information can overload the map with too much information for the reader to process.

trans-unit.source: Single Labels and Rule-based Labeling
trans-unit.target: Single Labels and Rule-based Labeling

trans-unit.source: QGIS offers two methods to display labels: __Single Labels__ and __Rule-based Labeling__
trans-unit.target: QGIS offers two methods to display labels: __Single Labels__ and __Rule-based Labeling__

trans-unit.source: Single Labels
trans-unit.target: Single Labels

trans-unit.source: Creates a single label style for every feature in the layer. You can select an attribute (value) which will be displayed. For example, the name of a settlement. You need to know which attribute displays the information you want to display. Look at the attribute table of the dataset to find it out.
trans-unit.target: Creates a single label style for every feature in the layer. You can select an attribute (value) which will be displayed. For example, the name of a settlement. You need to know which attribute displays the information you want to display. Look at the attribute table of the dataset to find it out.

trans-unit.source: Single labels for each administrative region (adm1) in Nigeria. The reader is able to assign each label to the respective administrative entity. :::
trans-unit.target: Single labels for each administrative region (adm1) in Nigeria. The reader is able to assign each label to the respective administrative entity. :::

trans-unit.source: Assigning the correct attribute value in the labeling options. QGIS needs to know which attribute (column) of the attribute table should be displayed as a label. In this case, we want the name of the administrative region (`ADM1_EN`) to be displayed. :::
trans-unit.target: Assigning the correct attribute value in the labeling options. QGIS needs to know which attribute (column) of the attribute table should be displayed as a label. In this case, we want the name of the administrative region (`ADM1_EN`) to be displayed. :::

trans-unit.source: Adding Single Labels to a Layer
trans-unit.target: Adding Single Labels to a Layer

trans-unit.source: In the styling panel, click on the `Labels`-tab underneath the Symbology tab.
trans-unit.target: In the styling panel, click on the `Labels`-tab underneath the Symbology tab.

trans-unit.source: Select ![](../../fig/en_30.30.2_icon_single_labels) `Single labels`.
trans-unit.target: Select ![](../../fig/en_30.30.2_icon_single_labels) `Single labels`.

trans-unit.source: `Value` is where you choose the attribute that will be displayed as a label. For example `*ADM1_EN*` will display the English names of Nigerian states for each feature in the data set.
trans-unit.target: `Value` is where you choose the attribute that will be displayed as a label. For example `*ADM1_EN*` will display the English names of Nigerian states for each feature in the data set.

trans-unit.source: Let's __change the font__: Open the font dropdown menu and select Arial. Make the text `Bold` in the Style dropdown menu. Change the colour by clicking on `Colour`, and change the `Size` to 8 pt
trans-unit.target: Let's __change the font__: Open the font dropdown menu and select Arial. Make the text `Bold` in the Style dropdown menu. Change the colour by clicking on `Colour`, and change the `Size` to 8 pt

trans-unit.source: Let's __add a white buffer__ around the label. In the `Labels` tab, you will find a list with different options to style the labels. Right now, we are in the `Text` menu. Select `Buffer` and check the `Draw text buffer` option. This will make the labels stand out more on dark or crowded maps.
trans-unit.target: Let's __add a white buffer__ around the label. In the `Labels` tab, you will find a list with different options to style the labels. Right now, we are in the `Text` menu. Select `Buffer` and check the `Draw text buffer` option. This will make the labels stand out more on dark or crowded maps.

trans-unit.source: Click `Apply` and `OK`.
trans-unit.target: Click `Apply` and `OK`.

trans-unit.source: Setting up labels in QGIS 30.30.2 :::
trans-unit.target: Setting up labels in QGIS 30.30.2 :::

trans-unit.source: Single Labels are not always useful. For example, if the dataset is too big, or you only want to display certain features in the dataset. In the example below, there are too many settlements to display labels for each settlement. Instead, it might be useful to only display the regional and national capitals. For such a use case, Rule-based Labeling is ideal.
trans-unit.target: Single Labels are not always useful. For example, if the dataset is too big, or you only want to display certain features in the dataset. In the example below, there are too many settlements to display labels for each settlement. Instead, it might be useful to only display the regional and national capitals. For such a use case, Rule-based Labeling is ideal.

trans-unit.source: Single Labels were selected to display the names of the settlements (red dots). A map with so much text information is unreadable and the information can hardly be understood. :::
trans-unit.target: Single Labels were selected to display the names of the settlements (red dots). A map with so much text information is unreadable and the information can hardly be understood. :::

trans-unit.source: Rule-based Labelling
trans-unit.target: Rule-based Labelling

trans-unit.source: Create rules using expressions to select accurately which features are to be labeled. Each rule can have a different text formatting. Use this if you want to have more control over the information that will be displayed as labels. For example, you can filter your data to only display the names of regional capitals.
trans-unit.target: Create rules using expressions to select accurately which features are to be labeled. Each rule can have a different text formatting. Use this if you want to have more control over the information that will be displayed as labels. For example, you can filter your data to only display the names of regional capitals.

trans-unit.source: Rule-based labeling allows you to filter datasets. This way, you can display the labels only for selected features without altering the dataset. :::
trans-unit.target: Rule-based labeling allows you to filter datasets. This way, you can display the labels only for selected features without altering the dataset. :::

trans-unit.source: The rules, or filters, are based on an expression. You can use the ![](../../fig/expression_string_builder_icon.png) `Expression string builder` to the right of the __Filter__ option in the label panel.
trans-unit.target: The rules, or filters, are based on an expression. You can use the ![](../../fig/expression_string_builder_icon.png) `Expression string builder` to the right of the __Filter__ option in the label panel.

trans-unit.source: Adding Rule-based Labels to a Layer
trans-unit.target: Adding Rule-based Labels to a Layer

trans-unit.source: In the styling panel, click on the `Labels` tab underneath the Symbology tab.
trans-unit.target: In the styling panel, click on the `Labels` tab underneath the Symbology tab.

trans-unit.source: Select ![](/../fig/30.30.2_Icon_rule_based_labeling.png) `Rule-based Labeling`.
trans-unit.target: Select ![](/../fig/30.30.2_Icon_rule_based_labeling.png) `Rule-based Labeling`.

trans-unit.source: Add a Rule by clicking on the `+`-button in the left corner of the styling panel. A new window will open in the styling panel. In this window, you will enter the rule (`Filter`) and customize the label font, size, and placement. Additionally, you can enter a description.
trans-unit.target: Add a Rule by clicking on the `+`-button in the left corner of the styling panel. A new window will open in the styling panel. In this window, you will enter the rule (`Filter`) and customize the label font, size, and placement. Additionally, you can enter a description.

trans-unit.source: Enter a Filter (red box in the figure below). The easiest way is to use the `Expression string builder` to the right of the Filter option. Click on the ![](/../fig/expression_string_builder_icon.png)-Symbol. A new panel will open.
trans-unit.target: Enter a Filter (red box in the figure below). The easiest way is to use the `Expression string builder` to the right of the Filter option. Click on the ![](/../fig/expression_string_builder_icon.png)-Symbol. A new panel will open.

trans-unit.source: In the Expression String builder enter a rule. In the example in the video below, we want to only display settlements that are either national or regional capitals. This corresponds to the String `("CLASS" = 1 ) OR ("CLASS" = 2)`. We know this because we know our data and have looked at the attribute table beforehand.
trans-unit.target: In the Expression String builder enter a rule. In the example in the video below, we want to only display settlements that are either national or regional capitals. This corresponds to the String `("CLASS" = 1 ) OR ("CLASS" = 2)`. We know this because we know our data and have looked at the attribute table beforehand.

trans-unit.source: Click `OK`.
trans-unit.target: Click `OK`.

trans-unit.source: Set the font and font size.
trans-unit.target: Set the font and font size.

trans-unit.source: Click `Apply`.
trans-unit.target: Click `Apply`.

trans-unit.source: Below are some further considerations to keep in mind when using labels:
trans-unit.target: Below are some further considerations to keep in mind when using labels:

trans-unit.source: Only display information that serves the purpose of the map or is helpful to the reader. Useful information can be the name of a settlement or a place, so the reader can assign a certain symbol on the map to this particular place.
trans-unit.target: Only display information that serves the purpose of the map or is helpful to the reader. Useful information can be the name of a settlement or a place, so the reader can assign a certain symbol on the map to this particular place.

trans-unit.source: If you want to display different types of information as labels, the font needs to be different so the reader can differentiate between the different types of information that is displayed. A good practice is to display the labels in a similar colour to the objects it is referring to. For example, dark blue text for the labels of light blue bodies of water, or brown text for the labels of light-brown houses.
trans-unit.target: If you want to display different types of information as labels, the font needs to be different so the reader can differentiate between the different types of information that is displayed. A good practice is to display the labels in a similar colour to the objects it is referring to. For example, dark blue text for the labels of light blue bodies of water, or brown text for the labels of light-brown houses.

trans-unit.source: A good example of label placement and font. Pay attention to the text colours and orientation. Every label can easily be attributed to the correct cartographic feature. (Source: [Axis Maps](https://www.axismaps.com/guide/labeling)) :::
trans-unit.target: A good example of label placement and font. Pay attention to the text colours and orientation. Every label can easily be attributed to the correct cartographic feature. (Source: [Axis Maps](https://www.axismaps.com/guide/labeling)) :::

trans-unit.source: In most cases, displaying numerical values as labels is confusing to the reader and makes the map too complex. In most cases, for numerical data, you can choose a different visualization such as colours or symbol size.
trans-unit.target: In most cases, displaying numerical values as labels is confusing to the reader and makes the map too complex. In most cases, for numerical data, you can choose a different visualization such as colours or symbol size.

trans-unit.source: Numerical Labels :::
trans-unit.target: Numerical Labels :::

trans-unit.source: [Graduated Symbology](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Module_3/en_qgis_data_classification.html#graduated-classification) :::
trans-unit.target: [Graduated Symbology](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Module_3/en_qgis_data_classification.html#graduated-classification) :::

trans-unit.source: QGIS places the labels automatically. Sometimes, if you are using a lot of black outlines or dark colours, black text is hard to read on the map. In that case, you can add white buffer around the text to make it visible.
trans-unit.target: QGIS places the labels automatically. Sometimes, if you are using a lot of black outlines or dark colours, black text is hard to read on the map. In that case, you can add white buffer around the text to make it visible.

trans-unit.source: A label without a text buffer (left) and a label with a white text buffer (right). :::
trans-unit.target: A label without a text buffer (left) and a label with a white text buffer (right). :::

trans-unit.source: By default, QGIS renders the labels so that they don't overlap with other labels. This means that not all the labels will be visible if the data is dense or rendered close to each other. You can optimize the rendering under the rendering option.
trans-unit.target: By default, QGIS renders the labels so that they don't overlap with other labels. This means that not all the labels will be visible if the data is dense or rendered close to each other. You can optimize the rendering under the rendering option.

trans-unit.source: Check out the [wiki article](/content/Wiki/en_qgis_representation_wiki.md) for detailed, step-by-step tutorials on how to use the different features of the styling panel.
trans-unit.target: Check out the [wiki article](/content/Wiki/en_qgis_representation_wiki.md) for detailed, step-by-step tutorials on how to use the different features of the styling panel.

trans-unit.source: You can also read further in the article "[Labeling and text hierarchy in cartography](https://www.axismaps.com/guide/labeling)" by Axis Maps.
trans-unit.target: You can also read further in the article "[Labeling and text hierarchy in cartography](https://www.axismaps.com/guide/labeling)" by Axis Maps.

trans-unit.source: Self-Assessment Questions
trans-unit.target: Self-Assessment Questions

trans-unit.source: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note
trans-unit.target: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note

trans-unit.source: __When using Single Labels, what must you choose in order for labels to be displayed (i.e. what does “Value” refer to)?__
trans-unit.target: __When using Single Labels, what must you choose in order for labels to be displayed (i.e. what does “Value” refer to)?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: In Single Labels mode, you must pick an __attribute field__ (column) from the layer whose values will be shown as the labels. This is called the Value field. For example, you can choose the column storing the name of a street to display streetnames on your map.
trans-unit.target: In Single Labels mode, you must pick an __attribute field__ (column) from the layer whose values will be shown as the labels. This is called the Value field. For example, you can choose the column storing the name of a street to display streetnames on your map.

trans-unit.source: You can also use an expression instead of a simple field, e.g. concatenate multiple fields or include functions, to build what the label shows.
trans-unit.target: You can also use an expression instead of a simple field, e.g. concatenate multiple fields or include functions, to build what the label shows.

trans-unit.source: __What is a “rule” in rule‑based labeling, and how do you build one (e.g. using expressions)?__
trans-unit.target: __What is a “rule” in rule‑based labeling, and how do you build one (e.g. using expressions)?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: A __rule__ in rule‑based labeling is a condition (filter) that determines which features get labeled under that rule, and optionally with particular styling. Each rule can have its own label style (font, size etc.)
trans-unit.target: A __rule__ in rule‑based labeling is a condition (filter) that determines which features get labeled under that rule, and optionally with particular styling. Each rule can have its own label style (font, size etc.)

trans-unit.source: To build a rule: in the layer’s Label properties, select Rule‑based labeling. Then add a new rule, give it a description, and set a filter expression (using the expression builder) that tests some attribute(s) (and optionally scale, visibility etc.). Only features satisfying the expression are labeled under that rule. :::
trans-unit.target: To build a rule: in the layer’s Label properties, select Rule‑based labeling. Then add a new rule, give it a description, and set a filter expression (using the expression builder) that tests some attribute(s) (and optionally scale, visibility etc.). Only features satisfying the expression are labeled under that rule. :::

trans-unit.source: __Why might you prefer rule‑based labeling instead of labeling every feature (using Single Labels)? Give an example scenario.__
trans-unit.target: __Why might you prefer rule‑based labeling instead of labeling every feature (using Single Labels)? Give an example scenario.__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: __Rule‑based labeling__ lets you label selectively (only some features, e.g. only major roads, only capital cities, or features above a certain size), avoiding clutter.
trans-unit.target: __Rule‑based labeling__ lets you label selectively (only some features, e.g. only major roads, only capital cities, or features above a certain size), avoiding clutter.

trans-unit.source: It also lets you vary label styling per rule (different font size, color, style) depending on the feature’s attribute or scale.
trans-unit.target: It also lets you vary label styling per rule (different font size, color, style) depending on the feature’s attribute or scale.

trans-unit.source: __Example scenario__: Suppose you have a map of cities, and you want to label only large cities (population > 100,000) with their names in big bold font, medium cities in smaller font, and omit very small towns to reduce crowding. You can make rules like "population" > 100000, 10000 < "population" <= 100000, etc. Each rule labels only those features, styled appropriately. This avoids labeling every little town which could overlap and clutter. :::
trans-unit.target: __Example scenario__: Suppose you have a map of cities, and you want to label only large cities (population > 100,000) with their names in big bold font, medium cities in smaller font, and omit very small towns to reduce crowding. You can make rules like "population" > 100000, 10000 < "population" <= 100000, etc. Each rule labels only those features, styled appropriately. This avoids labeling every little town which could overlap and clutter. :::

trans-unit.source: __Why is it generally discouraged to label numerical (continuous) values directly as text labels?__
trans-unit.target: __Why is it generally discouraged to label numerical (continuous) values directly as text labels?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: Continuous numerical values (e.g. precise counts, measurements) tend to produce __too many unique labels__, which can clutter the map or overlap, making it hard to read.
trans-unit.target: Continuous numerical values (e.g. precise counts, measurements) tend to produce __too many unique labels__, which can clutter the map or overlap, making it hard to read.

trans-unit.source: They often change continuously, so small changes (minor precision) may not be useful and can overwhelm the labeling.
trans-unit.target: They often change continuously, so small changes (minor precision) may not be useful and can overwhelm the labeling.

trans-unit.source: Also, continuous values are better represented via __graded visual variables__ (e.g. graduated color, size, or choropleth) rather than text, so viewers perceive relative magnitude more naturally. :::
trans-unit.target: Also, continuous values are better represented via __graded visual variables__ (e.g. graduated color, size, or choropleth) rather than text, so viewers perceive relative magnitude more naturally. :::

trans-unit.source: Further Resources
trans-unit.target: Further Resources

trans-unit.source: [IFRC brand-compliant fonts](https://learn-sims.org/style-guidance/standard-fonts/)
trans-unit.target: [IFRC brand-compliant fonts](https://learn-sims.org/style-guidance/standard-fonts/)
